meta.question: Who funded Ridesharing Works for Austin in 2016?

event.question: Who funded Ridesharing Works for Austin in 2016?

event.description: Finding the filer record for Ridesharing Works for Austin

source.rowSummary: Austin City Clerk campaign finance, recipient Ridesharing Works for Austin: 71 reported contributions, $3,212,400.45 (2016-2016).

event.description: Retrieving top donors to Ridesharing Works for Austin in 2016

source.rowSummary: Austin City Clerk campaign finance, contribution: Uber Tech -> Ridesharing Works for Austin, $1,000,000, 04/28/2016.
Austin City Clerk campaign finance, contribution: Lyft, Inc. -> Ridesharing Works for Austin, $60,906.29, 06/29/2016.

event.text: Two companies bought one PAC. Uber and Lyft together put $3.21M into Ridesharing Works for Austin in 2016 — and Uber alone wrote 93% of it. The PAC reported 71 contributions; only two donors mattered.

citations.rowSummary: Austin City Clerk campaign finance, recipient Ridesharing Works for Austin: 71 reported contributions, $3,212,400.45 (2016-2016).
Austin City Clerk campaign finance, contribution: Uber Tech -> Ridesharing Works for Austin, $1,000,000, 04/28/2016.
Austin City Clerk campaign finance, contribution: Lyft, Inc. -> Ridesharing Works for Austin, $60,906.29, 06/29/2016.

event.text: What was on the ballot: Proposition 1, the May 2016 measure that would have rolled back Austin's fingerprint-based background-check rules for rideshare drivers. Uber and Lyft both opposed it. The PAC's full $3.21M war chest was raised, spent, and wound down inside that single cycle. Voters kept the rules; both companies pulled out of Austin within days, and the city ran on smaller competitors for a year.

citations.rowSummary: Austin City Clerk campaign finance, recipient Ridesharing Works for Austin: 71 reported contributions, $3,212,400.45 (2016-2016).

event.text: The two companies played different cards. Uber wrote 17 checks averaging $176K; its single $1M contribution on April 28 is the largest cycle-end transfer in the dataset. Lyft wrote 54 — three times as many — but its biggest was $60,906. Same cause, opposite donor profile: Uber went big in a few moves; Lyft drip-fed a longer ledger of mid-five-figure deposits.
